q: An electronic communication system is composed of the transmission, reception, and processing of information between two or more locations.
The basic components of an electronic communication system are the transmitter, communication channel, medium receiver and noise.
Noise is an unwanted signal that disturbs, interferes and affects the transmitted signal.
Information source is a device that generates messages or data such as voice, audio, music, video, or computer keyboard input.
A transmitter is the medium that connects the transmitter and receiver to convey information in a communication system.
The receiver reconstructs the transmitted signal and delivers it to the destination called user of information.
Noise can be prevented entirely by the appropriate design of the channel and shielding of cables.
Digital signals are more immune to noise than analog signals.
Telephony is an example of broadcast mode of communication while radio is an example of point-to-point.
Radio and television are examples of broadcast mode of communication involving a large number of receivers.
Analog communication conveys information including image, voice and video by using an analog signal which varies in amplitude, phase, or other properties with time.
Digital communication allows data to be broken into packets as discrete messages which is not allowed in analog communication.
In digital communication, transfer of data occurs in the form of a digital bit stream, i.e. 0 or 1 over a transmission medium.
In analog communication, transmission errors can be detected and corrected due to the nature of the continuous sinusoidal signal.
Wired (Line) communication requires a physical connection like co-axial cables, parallel wire lines, or optical fibre.
Optical fibre is a type of wireless (space) communication medium used for high-speed data transmission.
A transducer is a device that transforms a signal from one energy form to another energy form.
Common examples of transducers include loudspeakers, microphones, oximeters, and thermometers.
LEDs and antennas are not considered transducers because they do not transform signals between different energy forms.
Modulation is the process of changing characteristics like amplitude, frequency or phase of a carrier wave in accordance with the intensity of the signal.
The resultant wave after the process of modulation is called the sound wave and it contains the audio signal.
Modulation permits the transmission to occur at high frequency while simultaneously allowing the carrying of the audio signal.
There are three basic types of modulation: Amplitude modulation (AM), Frequency modulation (FM) and Phase modulation (PM).
In amplitude modulation (AM), the frequency of the modulated wave remains the same while the amplitude changes in accordance with signal intensity.
AM is widely used for broadcasting music and speech because it is more efficient than FM in higher frequency spectrums.
In frequency modulation (FM), only the frequency of the carrier wave is changed while the amplitude remains the same.
In frequency modulation (FM), the carrier frequency remains constant with modulation while the amplitude changes according to signal strength.
FM radio ranges in a higher spectrum from $88$ to $108 MHz$ while AM radio ranges from $535$ to $1705 kHz$.
Demodulation is the process of recovering the carrier signal from the modulated sound wave.
A simple radio receiver comprises roughly three pieces: the antenna, tuner, and speaker.
In a radio receiver, the rf amplifier is used to convert the signal back into sound.
Sound can travel very far and be transmitted over long distances without the use of radio waves or electronic circuits.
